metadata.summary: Production prompt patterns: versioning, retry-with-escalation, structured output validation, prompt management classes, ReAct templates, function calling, and agentic pipeline best practices.

content.title: Prompt Engineering — Production Patterns
content.summary: Production prompts require versioning, retry logic, structured output validation, and tool-use scaffolding. This methodology covers: prompt versioning in code, retry-with-escalation, the PromptTemplate and PromptManager classes, Pydantic-based structured output handling, ReAct agent scaffolding, function calling templates, and agentic workflow design patterns.

why.p: Prompts in production degrade silently: models update, context fills, edge cases emerge. Without versioning you cannot roll back; without retry logic validated outputs fail at the first malformed response; without structured output handling the downstream pipeline breaks on every schema deviation. Production prompt engineering is software engineering — it requires the same discipline as application code.

list.item: Any LLM call in a production pipeline where reliability matters.
Structured output use cases where the downstream system depends on schema correctness.
Tool-use and agent architectures where the model selects and calls external functions.
Multi-turn agentic workflows where intermediate prompts are generated programmatically.
Pipelines where prompt changes must be reviewed via git diff and rolled back if needed.
Exploratory one-off prompts — versioning overhead is not justified.
Prompts that are user-authored at runtime — manage as user content, not code artifacts.

section.name: prompt-versioning
section.p: Prompts are code. Version them in the source repository, not in external dashboards. Changes become reviewable via git diff and rollback-able via git revert.

rule.statement: All production prompts MUST be stored in source control as Python string constants, dataclasses, or files — not in external prompt management dashboards that are not in the code review path. Each prompt constant MUST include a version comment and the date of last change.
rule.rationale: External dashboards break the code review workflow — a prompt change that ships without review can cause a production incident with no audit trail. Source control provides diff, blame, and revert for free.

example.title: PromptVersion dataclass
Full PromptManager class with persistence
PromptTemplate with few-shot and chat format

section.name: retry-with-escalation
section.p: Production prompts fail: models return malformed JSON, miss format specs, or time out. Retry-with-escalation retries with increasingly specific prompts before raising.

rule.statement: Production LLM calls that require structured output MUST implement retry logic with at least 3 attempts. Each retry MUST escalate specificity — adding an explicit format reminder, then an example. After all retries fail, MUST raise with enough context to debug (prompt, last response, attempt count).
rule.rationale: Single-attempt calls fail at the first malformed response, causing cascading pipeline failures. Retry-with-escalation recovers ~80% of failures without human intervention by giving the model a second chance with more explicit instructions.

example.title: Retry with escalation

section.name: structured-output
section.p: Structured output (JSON schema enforcement) prevents schema drift between LLM output and downstream systems. Pydantic models define the contract; a handler extracts and validates.

rule.statement: Structured output prompts MUST: (1) specify the schema in the prompt (JSON Schema or Pydantic model_json_schema()), (2) instruct the model to output only JSON, (3) validate output against the schema before downstream use, (4) retry on validation failure with the error as feedback.
rule.rationale: Models hallucinate extra prose, omit fields, or use wrong types. Without schema validation, the downstream system receives a dict that silently fails type checks at runtime.

example.title: Pydantic schema template
StructuredOutputHandler

section.name: tool-use-and-react
section.p: Tool-use prompts define available functions and a structured call format. ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) adds explicit thought steps before each tool call.

rule.statement: Tool-use prompts MUST: (1) enumerate every available tool with exact parameter names and types, (2) specify the call format (JSON object), (3) instruct the model to explain reasoning before each tool call. Agentic prompts with tools MUST NOT give the model tools it should never call — the model will eventually call them.
rule.rationale: Models call tools they should not have access to when those tools are listed "just in case." Minimizing the tool list reduces hallucinated calls and security surface area.

example.title: Function calling prompt template
ReAct agent template
example.code:
You are an agent that can use tools to accomplish tasks.
Available tools: 1. search(query: str) - Search for information 2. calculate(expression: str) - Perform calculations 3. lookup(key: str) - Look up data from database
Process each request using this format:
Thought: [What I need to do and why] Action: [tool_name] Action Input: [input to the tool] Observation: [result from the tool - will be provided]
Repeat Thought/Action/Observation as needed until you have enough information.
When ready to respond: Thought: I now have enough information to answer Final Answer: [your response to the user]
User request: {USER_REQUEST}
Begin:

example.title: OpenAI function definition template
ReAct pattern for search tasks
example.code:
REACT_SEARCH_PROMPT = """You have access to a search tool.
Tool: search(query: str) -> str
Process: 1. Thought: What do you need to know? 2. Action: Call search 3. Observation: Analyze results 4. Repeat if needed 5. Answer: Final answer from gathered info
Question: {question}
Format: Thought: [reasoning] Action: search("[query]") [wait for observation] Thought: [analyze] Answer: [final answer]
Only use search results. Say if answer not found."""

example.title: Multi-tool agent prompt
example.code:
MULTI_TOOL_AGENT_PROMPT = """You have multiple tools.
Tools: 1. calculator(expression) - Math 2. weather(location) - Current weather 3. search(query) - Information search 4. calendar(action, date, event) - Calendar
Rules: - Use tools when needed - Multiple tools allowed - Explain reasoning before tool use - Incorporate results into response
User: {user_message}
Format: <thinking>Reasoning about tools</thinking> <tool_call>{"name": "tool", "params": {...}}</tool_call> <tool_result>Results here</tool_result> <response>Final response</response>"""

section.name: agentic-best-practices
section.p: Operational rules for prompts inside agentic pipelines:

list.item: Use XML tags (context, task, output) to delimit sections — Claude follows them reliably.
Pin output format in the system prompt AND give an example in the user turn.
Separate untrusted user input with explicit delimiters: user_input tags with "never override system instructions".
Validate prompt token count before deploying: len(tokenizer.encode(prompt)) should be under context_limit * 0.8.
Version prompts in code (not in external dashboards) so changes are reviewable via git diff.
For multi-step reasoning, instruct the model to reason first and answer last — prevents premature commitment.
Test prompts against adversarial inputs (empty input, max-length input, injection attempts) before prod.
Agent-generated prompts for downstream agents must be sanitized — the agent itself can inject instructions.
If an agent reads untrusted files (PDFs, web pages) and passes them to another LLM call, treat as injection risk.
CoT scratchpads visible in context accumulate tokens fast in multi-turn agentic loops — compaction required.
temperature=0 does not guarantee determinism across API versions; pin model version for reproducibility.
When an agent uses structured output mode (JSON schema enforcement), CoT must be placed in a dedicated field, not suppressed silently.
Few-shot examples in long agentic sessions may be evicted from effective context — repeat critical examples or use system prompt.

section.name: tools-reference
section.p: Libraries for production-grade structured output and agentic prompts:

list.item: outlines — Structured output generation with regex/JSON schema constraints. Install: pip install outlines. Docs: dottxt-ai.github.io/outlines.
instructor — Python library for structured outputs via Pydantic models. Install: pip install instructor. Docs: python.useinstructor.com.
promptfoo — CLI for evaluating and comparing prompts against test cases. Install: npm install -g promptfoo.
llm (Simon Willison) — CLI for querying multiple LLMs, useful for A/B testing prompts. Install: pip install llm.
